narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your Year 1 school decile rank of 3—below the typical student's 5.71—your strikingly low Year 3 decile rank of 1 compared to the average 5.53, a middle family income bracket similar to many applicants, an LSAT score of 18 that's much lower than the dataset average of 36.7, an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 right around the typical 3.21, your identity as a female applicant in a group that makes up about 44% of students, your Asian background which is less common at 6% versus 84% White students, and your plan to attend full-time like most at 93%. Among all these, your Year 3 decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the model firmly toward predicting bar exam failure because it signals weaker academic performance relative to peers. Your low LSAT score came in second, significantly reinforcing that risk by standing out as well below what successful bar takers usually achieve. Your Year 1 decile rank followed closely, adding to the concern over early inconsistent grades. Even your solid undergraduate GPA, family income, racial background, gender, and full-time plans each played smaller but still contributing roles, all nudging the prediction toward failure when compared to the stronger profiles of students who typically pass. In essence, your situation stands apart from the average applicant because your school deciles and LSAT are notably lower, while your other factors align more closely with the norm. To turn things around for future applications or bar prep, focus on what you can realistically improve, like retaking the LSAT to boost that critical score toward the typical 37 range through dedicated practice. Building stronger late-stage academic habits, as shown by your Year 3 decile, could help via additional coursework or certifications that demonstrate growth. With targeted bar exam preparation tailored to your full-time schedule, you could outperform this prediction and show admissions committees your potential. Keep pushing forward—you have average strengths like your GPA to build on, and many students overcome early hurdles with persistence.
